headline: Robots Clock In Beyond the Warehouse
deck: A humanoid works a gate at Haneda, Figure units log hours on BMW's line, and a robot fleet just finished a 100-megawatt solar farm. Bank of America sees shipments jumping from tens of thousands this year toward millions by 2030. The demos are becoming shifts.
body: The humanoid is turning up on payroll, not just on stage. Japan Airlines put two Unitree robots to work at Haneda Airport in May, BMW's Figure 02 units have logged roughly 1,250 operational hours and contributed to more than 30,000 vehicles at Spartanburg, and Amazon runs Agility's Digit in live fulfilment, according to a survey of 2026 deployments [E1]. Each of those is a shift worked, not a press-conference stunt [E1].

The work is also moving outdoors. A robot fleet from Maximo completed a 100-megawatt solar installation, and Toyota's research arm is customising NVIDIA's Cosmos world models to train machines for physical tasks, per NVIDIA's own accounting of the field [E2]. Utility-scale construction and factory lines are a longer way from a lab than a warehouse aisle [E2].

Production is trying to keep pace. Unitree shipped about 5,500 units in 2025 and is targeting 20,000 this year, and China's AgiBot said it built its 10,000th humanoid by late March, a run-rate that turns the category from prototypes into inventory [E1]. That is the pilot-to-platform shift the sector has promised: isolated trials hardening into standing deployments [E1].

The forecasts stretch far past the shop floor. Bank of America projects roughly 90,000 humanoid shipments in 2026 rising to about 1.2 million by 2030, and Morgan Stanley puts the addressable market near $5 trillion with a billion humanoids deployed by 2050 [E1]. Those are scenarios, not order books, and the gap between the two is the whole risk [E1].

The nulls are worth holding. Operational hours and one solar farm are evidence of capability, not of an installed base, and multi-decade population curves compound small assumptions into enormous numbers [E1][E2]. The discriminators are dull and decisive, audited fleet-hours, repeat orders, and cost per task, not launch-day tallies. Robots are clocking in; the question the desk will track is whether they clock in again next quarter [E1].

dissent.argument: A billion-unit forecast to 2050 is close to unfalsifiable, and pilot hours convert to revenue slowly. The durable signal is repeat industrial orders and cost-per-task, not deployment anecdotes or a $5 trillion market number.

evidence_box.fragment: 2026 humanoid deployments: JAL/Unitree at Haneda; BMW Figure 02 ~1,250 hours and 30,000+ vehicles; Amazon/Agility Digit; Unitree 5,500 in 2025 targeting 20,000; AgiBot 10,000th unit; BofA 90k shipments 2026 to 1.2m by 2030; Morgan Stanley $5tn TAM, 1bn by 2050
Physical-AI field deployments: Maximo robot fleet completed a 100-megawatt solar installation; Toyota Research customising Cosmos world foundation models; Isaac Sim/Lab across robotics startups
